title: Data Foundations: Types, Relational Databases & SQL
summary: Covers how data is classified, how relational databases structure and protect that data, and how SQL retrieves and aggregates it in an embassy context.
content:
Data at an embassy arrives in three structural forms. Structured data fits neatly into rows and columns with a fixed schema (a visa applications table, a payroll spreadsheet). Semi-structured data has tags or markers but no rigid table shape (JSON from a consular API, XML cables, log files, email headers). Unstructured data has no predefined model (PDF scans of passports, photos, free-text incident reports, video from a security feed). Roughly 80% of organizational data is unstructured, which is why classification drives storage and tooling choices.

A relational database stores structured data in tables (relations) made of rows (records) and columns (attributes/fields). A primary key uniquely identifies each row (e.g., a passport number or applicant ID) and cannot be NULL or duplicated. A foreign key is a column that references another table's primary key, enforcing referential integrity — you cannot record a visa for an applicant who does not exist.

• A natural key is a real-world identifier already meaningful in the data (passport number, email). A surrogate key is a system-generated value with no business meaning (an auto-increment ID or GUID). Surrogate keys are preferred when natural keys can change, repeat, or be long/composite — passports get reissued, so an internal applicant_id is safer.

Normalization removes redundancy to prevent update anomalies. 1NF: atomic values, no repeating groups (no 'phone1, phone2, phone3' columns). 2NF: 1NF plus every non-key column depends on the WHOLE composite key (no partial dependencies). 3NF: 2NF plus no non-key column depends on another non-key column (no transitive dependencies — store country_code, not the derived country_name in the same row). Denormalization deliberately reintroduces redundancy to speed up reads in reporting systems, trading storage and write-consistency for query performance.

Indexes are auxiliary structures (often B-trees) that speed up lookups and WHERE/JOIN filtering, like an index at the back of a book. They accelerate reads but slow writes and consume space, so you index the columns you frequently search, not every column.

ACID guarantees reliable transactions: Atomicity (all steps succeed or none do — a fee transfer either completes fully or rolls back), Consistency (the database moves only between valid states honoring all constraints), Isolation (concurrent transactions don't corrupt each other), Durability (committed data survives a crash). These properties matter when many consular officers update the same records simultaneously.

SQL is the language for relational data. SELECT chooses columns, FROM names the table, WHERE filters individual rows before grouping. GROUP BY collapses rows into groups for aggregate functions; HAVING filters those groups after aggregation. The key distinction: WHERE filters rows (cannot use aggregates), HAVING filters groups (can use aggregates like COUNT). Aggregates include COUNT (rows), SUM (total), AVG (mean) — note AVG and COUNT(column) ignore NULLs, while COUNT(*) counts all rows.

JOINs combine tables on related keys. INNER JOIN returns only matching rows in both tables. LEFT JOIN returns all rows from the left table plus matches from the right (NULLs where none match) — useful to find applicants with no appointment yet. RIGHT JOIN is the mirror image. UNION stacks the results of two queries vertically (combining rows, requiring matching columns), whereas a JOIN combines columns horizontally. NULL means 'unknown/absent', is not equal to zero or empty string, and requires IS NULL / IS NOT NULL rather than = NULL; arithmetic and most comparisons with NULL yield NULL.
keyPoints: Three data types: structured (tables/fixed schema), semi-structured (JSON/XML/logs with tags), unstructured (PDFs, images, free text); ~80% of data is unstructured
Primary key = unique, non-NULL row identifier; foreign key references another table's PK to enforce referential integrity
Natural key = real-world meaning (passport no.); surrogate key = system-generated, no meaning — preferred when natural keys change or repeat
1NF = atomic values/no repeating groups; 2NF = no partial dependency on composite key; 3NF = no transitive dependency between non-key columns
Denormalization adds redundancy for faster reads in reporting, trading write consistency and storage
Indexes speed reads/filtering but slow writes and use space; index frequently-queried columns only
ACID = Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability — guarantees reliable transactions
WHERE filters rows before grouping (no aggregates); HAVING filters groups after aggregation (allows aggregates)
INNER JOIN = matches only; LEFT JOIN = all left rows + matches (NULL fills); UNION stacks rows vertically vs JOIN combining columns horizontally
NULL = unknown/absent, not zero/empty; use IS NULL, not = NULL; AVG/COUNT(column) ignore NULLs, COUNT(*) counts all rows

flashcards.front: What are the three structural categories of data?
flashcards.back: Structured (rows/columns, fixed schema), semi-structured (tags/markers, e.g., JSON, XML, logs), and unstructured (no schema, e.g., PDFs, images, free text).
Primary key uniquely identifies each row (unique, not NULL). Foreign key references another table's primary key, enforcing referential integrity.

Natural key is a meaningful real-world identifier (passport number). Surrogate key is system-generated with no business meaning (auto-increment ID). Surrogate preferred when natural keys change/repeat.
1NF: atomic values, no repeating groups. 2NF: 1NF + no partial dependency on a composite key. 3NF: 2NF + no transitive dependency (non-key columns depend only on the key).

flashcards.front: What is denormalization and why use it?
flashcards.back: Deliberately reintroducing redundancy to speed up read/reporting queries, trading storage and write-consistency for performance.

flashcards.front: What does an index do, and its trade-off?
flashcards.back: Speeds up lookups, WHERE filtering, and JOINs (like a book index). Trade-off: slows writes and consumes extra storage.
Atomicity (all-or-nothing), Consistency (only valid states), Isolation (concurrent transactions don't interfere), Durability (committed data survives failures).
WHERE filters individual rows before grouping and can't use aggregates. HAVING filters groups after GROUP BY and can use aggregates like COUNT/SUM.
INNER returns only rows matching in both tables. LEFT returns all rows from the left table plus matching right rows, with NULLs where no match exists.
UNION stacks rows from two queries vertically (combines rows, columns must align). JOIN combines tables horizontally by adding columns on a key match.
NULL means unknown/absent — not zero or empty string. Use IS NULL / IS NOT NULL (not = NULL). Comparisons/arithmetic with NULL return NULL.
COUNT(*) counts all rows including NULLs; COUNT(column) counts only non-NULL values in that column. AVG and SUM also ignore NULLs.

flashcards.front: Order of evaluation: SELECT, WHERE, GROUP BY, HAVING
flashcards.back: Logically: FROM → WHERE (filter rows) → GROUP BY (form groups) → HAVING (filter groups) → SELECT (choose columns) → ORDER BY.

title: Beyond Relational: NoSQL, Pipelines & Warehousing
summary: Explains NoSQL database families and trade-offs, plus how data moves through pipelines into warehouses, lakes, and analytical schemas.
content:
NoSQL ('not only SQL') databases handle scale, flexible schemas, and non-tabular data. There are four main families. Document stores keep data as JSON-like documents (MongoDB) — good for consular case files with varying fields. Key-value stores map a key to a value for ultra-fast lookups (Redis) — good for session tokens and caching. Columnar (wide-column) stores group data by column for fast analytics over huge datasets (Cassandra, HBase). Graph databases store nodes and relationships (Neo4j) — ideal for mapping connections between people, organizations, and travel patterns in intelligence analysis.

The CAP theorem states a distributed system can guarantee at most two of three properties simultaneously: Consistency (every read sees the latest write), Availability (every request gets a response), and Partition tolerance (the system keeps working despite network splits). Because network partitions are unavoidable in distributed systems, the real choice is between consistency and availability during a partition.

• Sharding splits data horizontally across servers so each holds a subset (scaling capacity and write throughput). Replication copies the same data to multiple servers for fault tolerance and read scaling. They are complementary: shard to grow, replicate to survive failures.

Many NoSQL systems follow BASE instead of ACID: Basically Available, Soft state, Eventual consistency. Rather than guaranteeing immediate consistency, they accept that replicas converge over time — acceptable for a global directory where a brief lag is tolerable, but risky for financial ledgers.

Data pipelines move data from sources to destinations. ETL (Extract, Transform, Load) cleans and reshapes data before loading it into the warehouse — traditional, good when the target has limited compute. ELT (Extract, Load, Transform) loads raw data first and transforms it inside a powerful warehouse or lake — favored in modern cloud platforms. Change data capture (CDC) detects and streams only the rows that changed in a source system, enabling near-real-time syncs without reloading everything. Data lineage tracks data's origin and every transformation it undergoes — essential for auditing and trust ('where did this number come from?').

Storage targets differ by purpose. A data warehouse stores cleaned, structured, query-optimized data for reporting (schema-on-write). A data lake stores raw data of any type cheaply (schema-on-read), supporting future and machine-learning use cases. A data mart is a small, subject-specific slice of a warehouse for one department (e.g., a consular-affairs mart).

Analytical warehouses use dimensional modeling. A fact table holds measurable events and metrics (number of visas issued, fees collected) plus foreign keys; dimension tables hold descriptive context (date, country, visa type, post). A star schema places one central fact table directly connected to denormalized dimensions — simple and fast. A snowflake schema normalizes dimensions into sub-tables — less redundancy, more joins, slower.

Finally, OLTP vs OLAP describes workload type. OLTP (Online Transaction Processing) handles many short read/write transactions (recording each visa application) — normalized, ACID, current data. OLAP (Online Analytical Processing) handles complex aggregate queries over historical data (visa trends by region by year) — denormalized, read-heavy. Batch processing handles data in scheduled chunks (a nightly load); streaming processes events continuously as they arrive (real-time border alerts).
keyPoints: Four NoSQL families: document (MongoDB), key-value (Redis), columnar/wide-column (Cassandra), graph (Neo4j)
CAP theorem: a distributed system can guarantee only two of Consistency, Availability, Partition tolerance; partitions force a C-vs-A choice
Sharding splits data horizontally to scale; replication copies data for fault tolerance and read scaling — they are complementary
BASE (Basically Available, Soft state, Eventual consistency) is the NoSQL alternative to ACID — replicas converge over time
ETL transforms before loading (good for weak targets); ELT loads raw then transforms in a powerful warehouse (modern cloud default)
Change data capture (CDC) streams only changed rows for near-real-time sync; data lineage tracks origin and transformations for auditing
Warehouse = cleaned structured data (schema-on-write); lake = raw any-type data cheaply (schema-on-read); mart = department-specific warehouse slice
Fact tables hold metrics + foreign keys; dimension tables hold descriptive context
Star schema = central fact + denormalized dimensions (fast); snowflake = normalized dimensions (less redundancy, more joins)
OLTP = many short transactions, normalized, current; OLAP = complex aggregate analytics, denormalized, historical; batch vs streaming = scheduled chunks vs continuous events

flashcards.front: Name the four NoSQL database types with an example each.
flashcards.back: Document (MongoDB), key-value (Redis), columnar/wide-column (Cassandra), graph (Neo4j).

flashcards.front: What does the CAP theorem state?
flashcards.back: A distributed system can guarantee at most two of three: Consistency, Availability, Partition tolerance. Since partitions are unavoidable, you trade consistency vs availability.
Sharding splits data horizontally across servers to scale capacity/writes. Replication copies the same data to multiple servers for fault tolerance and read scaling.
Basically Available, Soft state, Eventual consistency — the NoSQL counterpart to ACID, accepting that replicas converge over time rather than being instantly consistent.
ETL transforms data before loading (good when the target has limited compute). ELT loads raw data first and transforms inside a powerful warehouse/lake (modern cloud approach).
A technique that detects and streams only the rows changed in a source, enabling near-real-time syncs without reloading the whole dataset.
A record of data's origin and every transformation it passes through, used for auditing, debugging, and trusting reported numbers.

flashcards.front: Data warehouse vs data lake vs data mart
flashcards.back: Warehouse: cleaned, structured, query-optimized (schema-on-write). Lake: raw, any-type, cheap (schema-on-read). Mart: small subject/department-specific slice of a warehouse.
Fact table stores measurable events/metrics plus foreign keys. Dimension tables store descriptive context (date, country, type).
Star: central fact table with denormalized dimensions (simpler, faster, more redundancy). Snowflake: dimensions normalized into sub-tables (less redundancy, more joins, slower).
OLTP: many short read/write transactions, normalized, current data (operations). OLAP: complex aggregate queries over historical data, denormalized (analytics/reporting).
Batch processes data in scheduled chunks (e.g., nightly load). Streaming processes events continuously as they arrive (e.g., real-time alerts).

flashcards.front: Why is graph data useful in diplomatic analysis?
flashcards.back: Graph databases model nodes and relationships, making them ideal for mapping connections between people, organizations, and travel/communication patterns.

title: Statistics, Visualization & Data Governance
summary: Covers core descriptive and inferential statistics, choosing the right chart, and the governance rules that keep embassy data trustworthy and protected.
content:
Descriptive statistics summarize a dataset. The mean is the arithmetic average (sensitive to outliers), the median is the middle value when sorted (robust to outliers), and the mode is the most frequent value. For skewed data — like a few very large fee payments — the median better represents the 'typical' value than the mean.

Spread measures how dispersed data is. Variance is the average of squared deviations from the mean; standard deviation is its square root, expressed in the original units, so it is easier to interpret. Percentiles split sorted data into 100 parts (the 90th percentile means 90% of values fall below it); quartiles split into four (Q1=25th, Q2=median, Q3=75th). The interquartile range (IQR = Q3 − Q1) captures the middle 50% and is a robust spread measure used to flag outliers — commonly any value below Q1 − 1.5×IQR or above Q3 + 1.5×IQR.

• A normal distribution is the symmetric bell curve where mean = median = mode. The empirical 68-95-99.7 rule states that about 68% of values fall within one standard deviation of the mean, 95% within two, and 99.7% within three — useful for judging how unusual a measurement is.

Key analytical cautions: outliers are extreme values that can distort the mean and models, so investigate (genuine signal vs data-entry error) before deleting. Correlation measures how two variables move together (from −1 to +1) but does not prove causation — a third confounding factor or coincidence may explain it ('correlation is not causation'). A moving average smooths short-term noise to reveal trends over time. Sampling bias occurs when the sample is not representative of the population (e.g., surveying only embassy visitors who used the website), producing misleading conclusions.

Inferential tools support decisions. A/B testing compares two versions (A vs B) with a randomized split to measure which performs better, using statistical significance to rule out chance. A confidence interval gives a range likely to contain the true population value at a stated confidence level (e.g., '95% confident the true average wait time is 18–22 minutes') — wider intervals mean more uncertainty.

Visualization choice follows the question. Use a line chart for trends over time; a bar chart to compare categories; a histogram to show the distribution/frequency of one numeric variable (bars touch, unlike a bar chart); a scatter plot to show the relationship/correlation between two numeric variables; a pie chart for parts of a whole (only a few categories); a box plot to show distribution spread, median, quartiles, and outliers at a glance; and a heatmap to show magnitude across two dimensions via color (e.g., applications by month and country).

Data governance keeps data fit for use. Data quality dimensions include accuracy, completeness, consistency, timeliness, validity, and uniqueness. The big-data '3 Vs' are Volume (amount), Velocity (speed of generation/processing), and Variety (range of formats) — sometimes extended with Veracity and Value.

Protecting people matters most in diplomatic work. PII (Personally Identifiable Information) is data that can identify an individual (name, passport number, biometrics). Anonymization irreversibly removes identifiers so individuals cannot be re-identified; pseudonymization replaces identifiers with reversible tokens (re-linkable with a separate key) — pseudonymized data is still considered personal data. A data dictionary documents each field's name, type, meaning, allowed values, and source, giving everyone a shared, authoritative definition of the data.
keyPoints: Mean is the average (outlier-sensitive); median is the middle value (robust); mode is the most frequent — use median for skewed data
Variance = average squared deviation; standard deviation = its square root in original units; IQR = Q3 − Q1 captures middle 50% and flags outliers (1.5×IQR rule)
Normal distribution is a symmetric bell curve where mean=median=mode; 68-95-99.7 rule covers ±1, ±2, ±3 standard deviations
Correlation (−1 to +1) measures association but does not prove causation; watch for confounders
Sampling bias = non-representative sample yields misleading results; moving averages smooth noise to reveal trends
A/B testing randomly splits to compare two versions; confidence intervals give a plausible range for the true value at a stated confidence level

Chart selection: line=trend over time, bar=compare categories, histogram=distribution of one numeric variable, scatter=relationship between two variables, pie=parts of whole, box plot=spread/quartiles/outliers, heatmap=magnitude by color across two dimensions
Data quality dimensions: accuracy, completeness, consistency, timeliness, validity, uniqueness
Big-data 3 Vs: Volume, Velocity, Variety (often extended with Veracity, Value)
PII identifies individuals; anonymization is irreversible, pseudonymization is reversible with a key (still personal data); a data dictionary documents each field

flashcards.front: Mean vs median vs mode — and which suits skewed data?
flashcards.back: Mean = average (sensitive to outliers); median = middle value (robust); mode = most frequent. Use the median for skewed data.
Variance is the average of squared deviations from the mean. Standard deviation is its square root, expressed in the original units, so it's easier to interpret.

flashcards.front: What is the IQR and how does it flag outliers?
flashcards.back: IQR = Q3 − Q1 (middle 50% spread). Outliers are commonly values below Q1 − 1.5×IQR or above Q3 + 1.5×IQR.
In a normal distribution, ~68% of values fall within ±1 standard deviation of the mean, ~95% within ±2, and ~99.7% within ±3.
Two variables moving together (correlation, −1 to +1) doesn't mean one causes the other — a confounding variable or coincidence may explain the link.
When the sample isn't representative of the target population, producing skewed, misleading conclusions (e.g., surveying only website visitors).
A range likely to contain the true population value at a stated confidence level (e.g., 95% confident the true mean wait is 18–22 min). Wider = more uncertainty.
Comparing two versions (A vs B) with a randomized split and statistical significance testing to determine which performs better while ruling out chance.

flashcards.front: When do you use a histogram vs a bar chart?
flashcards.back: Histogram shows the distribution/frequency of one numeric variable (bars touch). Bar chart compares values across distinct categories (bars separated).

flashcards.front: Which chart shows the relationship between two numeric variables?
flashcards.back: A scatter plot — it reveals correlation, clusters, and outliers between two numeric variables.

flashcards.front: What does a box plot display?
flashcards.back: The distribution at a glance: median, quartiles (Q1/Q3), IQR spread, whiskers, and outliers.
Volume (amount), Velocity (speed of generation/processing), Variety (range of formats). Sometimes extended with Veracity and Value.
Anonymization irreversibly removes identifiers (can't re-identify). Pseudonymization replaces identifiers with reversible tokens re-linkable via a key — still treated as personal data.

flashcards.front: What is PII and a data dictionary?
flashcards.back: PII is data that can identify an individual (name, passport number, biometrics). A data dictionary documents each field's name, type, meaning, allowed values, and source.
Accuracy, completeness, consistency, timeliness, validity, and uniqueness.
